content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 2, 2010

Mr. Steven T. Mattas
City Attorney, City of South San Francisco
Myers Nave Riback Silver and Wilson
575 Market Street, Suite 2600
San Francisco, California 94105

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-125

Dear Mr. Mattas:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and is based on the facts presented; the Fair
Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) ] FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our advice is limited to issues arising under the
Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as
common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

Does a disqualifying conflict of interest exist if Vice Mayor Kevin Mullin, who serves as
President of KM2 Communications, participates in a future City Council action amending the
City's Zoning Code in a manner that would extend the duration of previously issued conditional
use permits including one conditional use permit issued to Genentech?

CONCLUSION

Because of Vice Mayor Mullin's economic interest in Genentech as a source of income,
he would be disqualified from participating in a future Council decision about an application
filed by Genentech for the City to amend its zoning code to extend the term of CUPs within the

1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-125
Page No. 2

Genentech Master Plan District. Disqualification based on Genentech as a source of income
lasts for 12 months after receipt of income from that source.

FACTS

Vice Mayor Mullin serves as the President of KM2 Communications ("KM2"), a
multimedia production firm that specializes in public affairs programming and video production
services. He is one of two salaried employees of KM2. Vice Mayor Mullin owns a 50 percent
or greater interest in KM2 and has the power to direct or cause the direction of the management
and policies of KM2. You state that Vice Mayor Mullin has an investment interest in the
business entity KM2 worth $2,000 or more, and that KM2 is a small business entity under
Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).

Between May 27, 2009, and April 1, 2010, KM2 entered into four contracts with
Genentech USA, Inc. ("Genentech") to produce and provide training videos. KM2 earned a total
of $14,500 from the production of product sales training videos for Genentech. KM2 has not
done any further work for Genentech and will not do any further work for Genentech while Mr.
Mullin serves on the City Council.

Genentech is a privately held corporation that generates in excess of $10 billion dollars in
gross revenue, annually. In March of 2009, the Roche Group acquired Genentech pursuant to a
short-form merger. As part of their merger agreement, Roche and Genentech combined their
pharmaceutical operations in the United States. Genentech is presently a wholly-owned member
of the Roche Group ("Roche"). Roche is a Swiss company, whose securities are quoted on the
electronic SIX Swiss Exchange, as well as on the other European exchanges. In 2009, Roche
reported pharmaceutical sales of approximately 14.8 billion Swiss Francs ($12.7 Billion USD) in
the United States alone.

Genentech's headquarters are located within the jurisdictional limits of the City. In 2007,
the City approved a ten year master plan for the South San Francisco Genentech campus, which
included the planned construction of a number of buildings. Many of the buildings require
conditional use permits ("CUP"), which Genentech has obtained for some of the proposed
buildings. On December 7, 2007, Genentech obtained approval for a CUP for Building FRC-3, a
five-story 278,600 square foot research and development complex, consisting of two buildings
and a bridge connection to an existing building, located at 330 Pt. San Bruno Boulevard. On
December 3, 2009, Genentech received approval by the South San Francisco Planning
Commission for a one year extension to the CUP, which will now expire on December 7, 2010.
Vice Mayor Mullin is not a member of the South San Francisco Planning Commission. To this
date, Genentech has not obtained building permits for Building FRC-3.

The City's zoning code provides that failure to obtain a building permit within two years
of final approval of a CUP will cause the CUP to expire. The applicant can apply for a one-year
extension of the CUP, so that the CUP can remain valid for up to three years from the original

File No. A-10-125
Page No. 3

date of final approval without obtaining a building permit. As stated above, Genentech applied
for and received a one-year extension of the CUP approval.

Genentech may file an application that the City consider amending its zoning code to
extend the term of previously granted and currently valid CUPs within the Genentech Master
Plan District to expire at the same time the master plan expires in 2017. The question thus
presented is whether Vice Mayor Mullin, whose company KM2 has received income from its
contract with Genentech, is precluded from participating in a future City Council decision to
amend the zoning code to extend the term of previously granted and currently valid CUPs.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for determining whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.

Steps 1 and 2: Is Vice Mayor Mullin a public official making, participating in
making, or influencing a governmental decision?

As a member of the South San Francisco City Council, Vice Mayor Mullin is a public
official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, he may not make, participate in making, or
otherwise use his official position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of his economic interests. Vice Mayor Mullin may be
called upon to consider whether to amend the zoning code to extend the term of previously granted
and valid CUPs within the Genentech Master Plan District to expire at the same time the master
plan expires, in 2017, rather than expiring within two years as usual. If this matter comes before
the City Council, Vice Mayor Mullin will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using
his official position to influence a governmental decision.

Step 3: What are Vice Mayor Mullin's economic interests?

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:

File No. A-10-125
Page No. 4


1. An economic interest in a business entity² in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));
2. An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
3. An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
4. An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4);
5. An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).

Economic interest in KM2: Vice Mayor Mullin is one of two salaried employees of KM2 Communications, serves as its president, and has an investment interest in KM2 worth $2,000 or more. Vice Mayor Mullin also owns a 50 percent or greater interest in KM2 and has power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of KM2. Accordingly, Councilmember Mullin has an economic interest in KM2 Communications under the Act, as a result of his investment interest, his position with the company, and the fact that the business is a source of income to him.

Economic interest in Genentech: Vice Mayor Mullin, as a greater than 10 percent owner of KM2, has an economic interest in any sources of income to KM2 in which his pro rata share of that income aggregates to $500 or more during the 12-month period before the decision.³ Because of his ownership interest in KM2, sources of income to KM2 are considered to be sources of income to him, when his share of the income equals $500 or more. In this case, Genentech's payment to KM2 for the production of product sales training videos for Genentech was $14,500 and Vice Mayor Mullin's share of this income from Genentech has aggregated at least $300 within the past 12 months. Therefore, Councilmember Mullin also has an economic interest in Genentech as a source of income under the Act.


² Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association."

³ This is based on the Act's definition of income in Section 82030, which provides that the "[i]ncome of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater."

File No. A-10-125
Page No. 5

Step 4: Are his economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source of income,
either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."

Under your facts, Genentech may file an application requesting that the City consider
amending its current zoning code to extend the term of previously granted and currently valid
CUPs within the Genentech Master Plan District to expire at the same time the master plan
expires in 2017.

Here, KM2 would not be directly involved in the decision. However, Vice Mayor
Mullin's source of income, Genentech, would be (1) initiating the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application, and (2) the subject of the proceeding because it
involves the issuance or renewal of a permit with Genentech. Therefore, Genentech would be
directly involved in the Council decision you describe.

Regulation 18704.1(b) then provides that if a business entity or source of income is
directly involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standards set forth in 18705.1(b) or
18705.3(a) apply. If the business entity or source of income is not directly involved, the
materiality standards set forth in 18705.1(c) or 18705.3(b) apply.

Step 5: Materiality Standard

Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to each economic interest.

Materiality standard applicable to KM2:

For a business entity not directly involved in the governmental decision, Regulation
18705.1(c) provides for materiality standards that vary according to the financial size of the
business. For relatively small businesses (those with net income of less than $500,000 for the
most recent fiscal year) the materiality standards set forth in Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) apply:

"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)(1)-(3), the
financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it
is reasonably foreseeable that:

File No. A-10-125
Page No. 6

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

Because you have indicated that KM2 is a relatively small business and it would
not be directly involved in the governmental decision that is the subject of your question,
this materiality standard would apply.

Materiality standard applicable to Genentech:

For a source of income that is a business entity and not directly involved in a
governmental decision, the same materiality standards applicable to business entities set
forth in Regulation 18705.1(c) apply (see above). (Section 18705.3(b)(1).) Again, this
standard would apply to Vice Mayor Mullin's business, KM2.

For a source of income that is directly involved in the governmental decision, such as
Genentech, Regulation 18705.3(a) sets forth a stricter materiality standard:

"(a) Directly involved sources of income. Any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official, and who is
directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is deemed material."

Step 6: Reasonably Foreseeable

An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made
depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be certain to be
considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Under your facts the governmental decision involves Genentech filing an application for
the City to amend its zoning code to extend the term of CUPs within the Genentech Master Plan
District. As to KM2, under the materiality standards set forth in Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) above,
it is not reasonably foreseeable that this Council decision on the CUPs would have a material
financial effect on KM2. As to Genentech, however, it is substantially likely that a decision to
extend the CUPs would have some financial effect on Genentech, and under Regulation
18705.3(a), this financial effect if deemed material.

File No. A-10-125
Page No. 7

Accordingly, a Council decision concerning extending the CUPs obtained by Genentech
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Genentech, a source of income
for Vice Mayor Mullin. Because of Vice Mayor Mullin's economic interest in Genentech as a
source of income, he would be disqualified from participating in this decision.

Recusal: Regulation 18702.5 provides that when a public official who holds an office
specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting,
then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type
of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as
discussed in regulation 18702.5(b) on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself;
and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal
interests, special rules found in Regulations 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.

Steps 7 and 8: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation

You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exception would be applicable here. Accordingly, we have not
provided an analysis involving these steps.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5560.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

HPW:jgl

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,

avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a

standard would apply to vice Mayor Mullin's business, KM2.

"(a) Directly involved sources of income. Any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official, and who is
directly involved in a decision before the official'^, og"n"y, is deemed material.,,
Step 6: Reasonably Foreseeable
An effect Llpon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Wirether the financial

Under your facts the governmental decision involves Genentech filing an application for
the City to amend its zoning code to extend the term of CUPs within the Genentech Master plan
District. As to KM2, under the materiality standards set forth in Regulation lg705.l(c)(a) above,
it is not reasonably foreseeable that this Council decision on the CUps would have a material
financial effect on KM2. As to Genentech, however, it is substantially likely,fro, o decision to
extend the CUPs would have some financial effect on Genentech, and under Regulation
18705.3(a), this financial effect if deemed marerial.
Accordingly, a Council decision conceming extending the CUPs obtained by Genentech
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Genentech, a source of income
economic interest to Vice Mayor Mullin. Because of Vice Mayor Mullin's economic interest in
Genentech as a source of income, he would be disqualified from participating in this decision.
Recusal: Regulation 18702.5 provides that when a public official who holds an office
specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a clecision noticed at a public meeting,
then he or she must: (l) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, oraliy identify "*.f, typ"
of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the ".ononli" intereit, as
discussed in regulation 18102.5(b) on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself;
and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal
interests, special rules found in Regulations 18702.5(c) and 19702.5(d)ipply.
steps 7 and 8: Pubtic Generally and Legaily Required participation
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exception would be applicable here- Accoidingly, we have not
provided an analysis involving these steps.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 2, 2010 Mr. Steven T. Mattas City Attorney, City of South San Francisco Myers Nave Riback Silver and Wilson 575 Market Street, Suite 2600 San Francisco, California 94105 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-125 Dear Mr. Mattas: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) ] FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our advice is limited to issues arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION Does a disqualifying conflict of interest exist if Vice Mayor Kevin Mullin, who serves as President of KM2 Communications, participates in a future City Council action amending the City's Zoning Code in a manner that would extend the duration of previously issued conditional use permits including one conditional use permit issued to Genentech? CONCLUSION Because of Vice Mayor Mullin's economic interest in Genentech as a source of income, he would be disqualified from participating in a future Council decision about an application filed by Genentech for the City to amend its zoning code to extend the term of CUPs within the 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-10-125 Page No. 2 Genentech Master Plan District. Disqualification based on Genentech as a source of income lasts for 12 months after receipt of income from that source. FACTS Vice Mayor Mullin serves as the President of KM2 Communications ("KM2"), a multimedia production firm that specializes in public affairs programming and video production services. He is one of two salaried employees of KM2. Vice Mayor Mullin owns a 50 percent or greater interest in KM2 and has the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of KM2. You state that Vice Mayor Mullin has an investment interest in the business entity KM2 worth $2,000 or more, and that KM2 is a small business entity under Regulation 18705.1(c)(4). Between May 27, 2009, and April 1, 2010, KM2 entered into four contracts with Genentech USA, Inc. ("Genentech") to produce and provide training videos. KM2 earned a total of $14,500 from the production of product sales training videos for Genentech. KM2 has not done any further work for Genentech and will not do any further work for Genentech while Mr.
